1:
"The elder unto the wellbeloved Gaius, whom I love in the truth." The witnesses note the apostle's loving address to Gaius. Gill notes "the elder" is the writer, the Apostle John, who styles himself so on account of his age and office, as in the preceding epistle; the person addressed is "the wellbeloved Gaius," loved in the truth. The witnesses note John writing as the elder to the wellbeloved Gaius, whom he loves in the truth; the affectionate salutation to a beloved fellow-believer — that John, opening this short personal letter, names himself "the elder" (a title of age and office, the same he used in his second epistle) and addresses "the wellbeloved Gaius" (a beloved Christian friend, distinguished by the apostle's warm regard) — "whom I love in the truth"; the witnesses note the ground and the mark of this love — John loves Gaius "in the truth" (a love founded on and bounded by the gospel truth, a sincere and spiritual affection, not a mere natural fondness), the kind of love that binds true believers in Christ; so that the letter opens with the warm, truth-grounded love of the aged apostle for a faithful friend; the elder unto the wellbeloved Gaius, whom I love in the truth. The witnesses note the elder unto the wellbeloved Gaius. The witnesses note whom I love in the truth.

The witnesses note the affectionate salutation to Gaius — John, the elder, loving him "in the truth"; a sincere, spiritual love founded on the gospel, binding true believers in Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the loving salutation to Gaius. The lesson the expositors draw is that John writes as the elder to his beloved friend Gaius, loving him in the truth. The witnesses note the elder: "the elder" — Gill notes John's title of age and office; the elder; the John; the age; the office; the the writer. The expositors observe the same title: the same title used in the second epistle; the same title; the the second epistle; the the elder; the John; the consistent. The witnesses press the wellbeloved Gaius: "unto the wellbeloved Gaius" — a beloved Christian friend; the wellbeloved Gaius; the beloved; the the friend; the regarded; the Gaius. The expositors draw the lesson on the love in the truth: "whom I love in the truth" — a love founded on the gospel truth; the love in the truth; the founded; the the gospel; the sincere; the spiritual. The witnesses observe the not natural fondness: not a mere natural fondness but a spiritual love; the not natural fondness; the spiritual; the sincere; the in the truth; the love. The expositors note the binds believers: the love that binds true believers in Christ; the binds believers; the true believers; the in Christ; the the bond; the love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the elder unto the wellbeloved Gaius, the whom I love in the truth, and the loving salutation. The verse thus records the loving salutation to Gaius — teaching that John writes as the elder to his beloved friend, loving him in the truth.
2:
"Beloved, I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health, even as thy soul prospereth." The witnesses note John's prayer for Gaius's prosperity and health. Gill notes John wishes that Gaius may prosper and succeed in all temporal affairs; and as such success depends on the blessing of God, it is to be wished and prayed for from him. Barnes notes the word rendered "I wish" means rather "I pray." The witnesses note John praying that Gaius may prosper and be in health, even as his soul prospers; the prayer for the friend's outward welfare measured by his thriving soul — that John, with tender affection, makes a prayerful wish for Gaius's whole welfare: "beloved, I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health" (that he may succeed in his temporal affairs and enjoy bodily health) — "even as thy soul prospereth"; the witnesses note the high implied commendation — John wishes Gaius's outward state to match his inward, "even as thy soul prospereth," which takes for granted that Gaius's soul was in a flourishing, thriving condition; so that the apostle prays his friend may be as healthy and prosperous in body and estate as he already was in his soul — a wish that would be a poor thing for many, whose souls fare worse than their bodies, but a rich blessing for the spiritually thriving Gaius; even as thy soul prospereth. The witnesses note I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health. The witnesses note even as thy soul prospereth.

The witnesses note John's prayer for Gaius's prosperity and health measured by his thriving soul — wishing his outward state to match his flourishing inward one; a high implied commendation.

So the witnesses make the verse the prayer for welfare measured by the soul. The lesson the expositors draw is that John prays Gaius may prosper in body and estate even as his soul already prospers. The witnesses note the wish: "I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper" — Barnes notes "I pray"; the wish; the I pray; the prosper; the succeed; the temporal. The expositors observe the be in health: "and be in health" — and enjoy bodily health; the be in health; the bodily health; the the body; the welfare; the well. The witnesses press the even as thy soul: "even as thy soul prospereth" — as his soul thrives; the even as thy soul; the the soul; the thrives; the flourishing; the prospereth. The expositors draw the lesson on the implied commendation: this takes for granted his soul was thriving; the implied commendation; the takes for granted; the thriving; the flourishing; the the soul. The witnesses observe the outward match inward: John wishes his outward state to match his inward; the outward match inward; the match; the the inward; the the outward; the welfare. The expositors note the rich for the thriving: a rich wish for the spiritually thriving Gaius; the rich for the thriving; the spiritually thriving; the Gaius; the the soul; the rich. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health, the even as thy soul prospereth, and the prayer for welfare. The verse thus records the prayer for welfare measured by the soul — teaching that John prays Gaius may prosper in body even as his soul prospers.
3:
"For I rejoiced greatly, when the brethren came and testified of the truth that is in thee, even as thou walkest in the truth." The witnesses note John's joy at the good report of Gaius's truth. Gill notes the brethren came from the place where Gaius lived to where John resided; these seem to be preachers of the word who travelled to spread the gospel. Matthew Henry notes the good report the apostle received concerning his friend. The witnesses note John declaring that he rejoiced greatly when the brethren came and testified of the truth in Gaius, even as he walks in the truth; the apostle's great joy at the reported truth and consistent walk of Gaius — that John gives the ground of his confidence in Gaius's flourishing soul: "for I rejoiced greatly, when the brethren came and testified of the truth that is in thee" (the travelling brethren brought a good report, witnessing to the genuine truth and grace dwelling in Gaius) — "even as thou walkest in the truth"; the witnesses note the twofold testimony — not only "the truth that is in thee" (the truth received and held in the heart) but "as thou walkest in the truth" (the truth lived out and shown in his conduct), the inward truth proved by the outward walk; so that what gave John such great joy was the report that Gaius both held the truth and walked in it; I rejoiced greatly, when the brethren came and testified of the truth that is in thee. The witnesses note I rejoiced greatly, when the brethren came and testified of the truth that is in thee. The witnesses note even as thou walkest in the truth.

The witnesses note John's great joy at the report of Gaius — the brethren testifying to the truth in him and his walk in the truth; the inward truth proved by the outward walk.

So the witnesses make the verse John's joy at the reported truth and walk of Gaius. The lesson the expositors draw is that John rejoiced that Gaius both held the truth and walked in it, as the brethren testified. The witnesses note the rejoiced: "I rejoiced greatly, when the brethren came" — Gill notes travelling brethren brought a good report; the rejoiced; the the brethren; the the report; the travelling; the came. The expositors observe Matthew Henry's note: Henry notes the good report John received; the good report; the John received; the his friend; the testified; the the report. The witnesses press the truth in thee: "testified of the truth that is in thee" — the truth held in the heart; the truth in thee; the held; the the heart; the genuine; the received. The expositors draw the lesson on the walkest in truth: "even as thou walkest in the truth" — the truth lived out in conduct; the walkest in truth; the lived out; the conduct; the the walk; the shown. The witnesses observe the twofold testimony: truth in him and truth in his walk; the twofold testimony; the in him; the in his walk; the inward; the outward. The expositors note the proved by the walk: the inward truth proved by the outward walk; the proved by the walk; the inward; the outward; the the walk; the genuine. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I rejoiced greatly when the brethren came and testified of the truth that is in thee, the even as thou walkest in the truth, and John's joy. The verse thus records John's joy at the reported truth and walk of Gaius — teaching that John rejoiced that Gaius both held and walked in the truth.
4:
"I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth." The witnesses note the apostle's chief joy — his children walking in truth. Barnes notes there is no greater joy than to hear that one's children adhere steadfastly to the truth and live in accordance with it. Gill notes nothing causes greater joy than this. The witnesses note John declaring that he has no greater joy than to hear that his children walk in truth; the highest joy of the spiritual father — to see his children walking in the truth — that John tells the spring of his great rejoicing: "I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth"; the witnesses note the depth of this feeling — John, the aged apostle and spiritual father of many converts ("my children," those begotten through his ministry), counts no joy greater than to hear that they "walk in truth" (hold the truth and live by it); so that the faithful walk of his spiritual children was the apostle's deepest delight, dearer to him than any earthly joy; and herein he sets the pattern for every faithful minister and parent in the gospel, whose highest happiness is the godliness of those in their care; I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth. The witnesses note I have no greater joy. The witnesses note than to hear that my children walk in truth.

The witnesses note the apostle's chief joy — to hear his spiritual children walk in truth; the faithful walk of his children dearer to him than any earthly joy, the pattern for every gospel minister.

So the witnesses make the verse the highest joy of the spiritual father. The lesson the expositors draw is that John counts no joy greater than to hear his spiritual children walk in the truth. The witnesses note the no greater joy: "I have no greater joy" — Gill notes nothing causes greater joy; the no greater joy; the nothing greater; the the spring; the rejoicing; the delight. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes no greater joy than children adhering to truth; the Barnes; the adhering; the the truth; the live by it; the joy. The witnesses press the my children: "my children" — those begotten through his ministry; the my children; the begotten; the his ministry; the converts; the spiritual father. The expositors draw the lesson on the walk in truth: "walk in truth" — hold the truth and live by it; the walk in truth; the hold the truth; the live by it; the the walk; the godly. The witnesses observe the deepest delight: their faithful walk was his deepest delight; the deepest delight; the faithful walk; the dearer; the earthly joy; the his children. The expositors note the the pattern: the pattern for every faithful minister and parent; the the pattern; the every minister; the parent; the godliness; the in their care. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I have no greater joy, the than to hear that my children walk in truth, and the highest joy. The verse thus records the highest joy of the spiritual father — teaching that John counts no joy greater than his children walking in truth.
5:
"Beloved, thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren, and to strangers;" The witnesses note Gaius's faithful kindness to the brethren and strangers. Gill notes Gaius did a faithful thing, as became a faithful man, a believer in Christ; in all his beneficence he acted uprightly, not in a hypocritical way to be seen of men, but from a principle of love and with a view to the glory of God. The witnesses note John commending Gaius that he does faithfully whatsoever he does to the brethren and to strangers; the faithful, sincere hospitality of Gaius to known and unknown believers — that John turns to praise Gaius's particular grace, his hospitality: "beloved, thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren, and to strangers"; the witnesses note two things commended — first, the manner, "faithfully" (Gaius did his good works sincerely, from a true heart and a right principle, not for show, but as a faithful believer acting uprightly before God) — and second, the breadth, "to the brethren, and to strangers" (his kindness reached not only the believers he knew, but also the "strangers," the travelling brethren he had never met before); so that Gaius's hospitality was both genuine in its spring and wide in its reach, embracing known and unknown alike for Christ's sake; thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren, and to strangers. The witnesses note thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren. The witnesses note and to strangers.

The witnesses note Gaius's faithful, sincere hospitality — done not for show but from a true heart, reaching not only the brethren he knew but the travelling strangers; genuine in spring and wide in reach.

So the witnesses make the verse the faithful hospitality of Gaius. The lesson the expositors draw is that Gaius did his kindness sincerely and to both the known brethren and the unknown strangers. The witnesses note the faithfully: "thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest" — Gill notes done sincerely, from a true heart; the faithfully; the sincerely; the a true heart; the not for show; the uprightly. The expositors observe the not hypocritical: not in a hypocritical way to be seen of men; the not hypocritical; the not for show; the not seen of men; the from love; the the glory of God. The witnesses press the to the brethren: "to the brethren" — the believers he knew; the to the brethren; the the believers; the known; the the saints; the kindness. The expositors draw the lesson on the to strangers: "and to strangers" — the travelling brethren never met; the to strangers; the travelling; the never met; the unknown; the reached. The witnesses observe the genuine and wide: genuine in spring and wide in reach; the genuine and wide; the genuine; the wide; the the spring; the the reach. The expositors note the for Christ's sake: embracing known and unknown for Christ's sake; the for Christ's sake; the known and unknown; the embraced; the the brethren; the hospitality. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren, the and to strangers, and the faithful hospitality. The verse thus records the faithful hospitality of Gaius — teaching that Gaius did his kindness sincerely and to both the known brethren and the unknown strangers.
6:
"Which have borne witness of thy charity before the church: whom if thou bring forward on their journey after a godly sort, thou shalt do well:" The witnesses note the public testimony to Gaius's charity and the call to help the brethren forward. Gill notes the brethren, coming to the church at Ephesus where John was, and being affected with Gaius's kindness, could not forbear speaking of it to his praise before the members of the church. The witnesses note John saying that the brethren bore witness of Gaius's charity before the church, and that if he brings them forward on their journey in a godly manner, he does well; the public report of Gaius's love and the charge to forward the travelling brethren — that John tells how Gaius's kindness was made known and what further he might do: "which have borne witness of thy charity before the church" (the brethren so helped publicly testified to Gaius's love and liberality before the assembled church) — "whom if thou bring forward on their journey after a godly sort, thou shalt do well"; the witnesses note the two parts — the report (his charity openly praised before the church) and the further duty (to "bring [them] forward on their journey," that is, to furnish and forward the travelling preachers on their way, supplying their needs, "after a godly sort," in a manner befitting God and the gospel); so that John commends what Gaius had done and gently urges him to continue forwarding the Lord's servants worthily; whom if thou bring forward on their journey after a godly sort, thou shalt do well. The witnesses note which have borne witness of thy charity before the church. The witnesses note whom if thou bring forward on their journey after a godly sort, thou shalt do well.

The witnesses note the public report of Gaius's charity before the church, and the charge to forward the travelling brethren on their way "after a godly sort," in a manner befitting God and the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the report of charity and the charge to forward the brethren. The lesson the expositors draw is that Gaius's love was praised before the church, and he is urged to forward the travelling preachers worthily. The witnesses note the borne witness: "which have borne witness of thy charity before the church" — Gill notes the brethren publicly testified; the borne witness; the testified; the publicly; the before the church; the his charity. The expositors observe the his love: his love and liberality openly praised; the his love; the liberality; the praised; the openly; the the church. The witnesses press the bring forward: "whom if thou bring forward on their journey" — furnish and forward them on their way; the bring forward; the furnish; the forward; the their journey; the supply. The expositors draw the lesson on the after a godly sort: "after a godly sort" — in a manner befitting God; the after a godly sort; the befitting God; the the gospel; the worthily; the manner. The witnesses observe the thou shalt do well: "thou shalt do well" — he does well in so doing; the thou shalt do well; the does well; the so doing; the commended; the duty. The expositors note the continue: John urges him to continue forwarding the Lord's servants; the continue; the forwarding; the the Lord's servants; the worthily; the urged. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the which have borne witness of thy charity before the church, the whom if thou bring forward on their journey after a godly sort thou shalt do well, and the report and charge. The verse thus records the report of charity and the charge to forward the brethren — teaching that Gaius's love was praised, and he is urged to forward the travelling preachers worthily.
7:
"Because that for his name's sake they went forth, taking nothing of the Gentiles." The witnesses note the reason the travelling brethren deserved support — they went out for Christ's name, taking nothing of the heathen. Gill notes that for his name's sake they went forth from Judea, either of their own accord to preach the gospel, or being driven out by the unbelieving Jews for professing the name of Christ. The witnesses note John giving the reason — that for Christ's name's sake the brethren went forth, taking nothing of the Gentiles; the self-denying preachers worthy of support because they served Christ and took nothing from the heathen — that John gives the ground of the duty to forward these brethren: "because that for his name's sake they went forth" (they had gone out, in service of Christ's name and gospel, to preach it abroad) "taking nothing of the Gentiles"; the witnesses note the double commendation — first, their motive and errand, "for his name's sake" (they laboured and travelled purely for Christ's honour and the spread of his gospel, not for gain) — and second, their self-denial, "taking nothing of the Gentiles" (they refused to take support from the unconverted heathen, that the gospel might be free of charge and above the suspicion of covetousness); so that, since they would take nothing from the world they served, the believers ought the more readily to support them; for his name's sake they went forth, taking nothing of the Gentiles. The witnesses note because that for his name's sake they went forth. The witnesses note taking nothing of the Gentiles.

The witnesses note the self-denying preachers worthy of support — going forth for Christ's name and taking nothing of the heathen, that the gospel be free of charge and above suspicion of gain.

So the witnesses make the verse the ground for supporting the self-denying preachers. The lesson the expositors draw is that the brethren went out for Christ's name and refused heathen support, so the believers ought the more to support them. The witnesses note the for his name's sake: "for his name's sake they went forth" — Gill notes they went out for Christ's name; the for his name's sake; the for Christ; the the gospel; the went forth; the to preach. The expositors observe the their errand: their motive and errand was Christ's honour; the their errand; the the motive; the Christ's honour; the the gospel; the not gain. The witnesses press the taking nothing: "taking nothing of the Gentiles" — refusing heathen support; the taking nothing; the refusing; the heathen support; the the Gentiles; the the unconverted. The expositors draw the lesson on the free of charge: that the gospel might be free of charge; the free of charge; the the gospel; the free; the no charge; the above suspicion. The witnesses observe the no covetousness: above the suspicion of covetousness; the no covetousness; the above suspicion; the not for gain; the self-denying; the pure. The expositors note the more readily support: the believers ought the more readily to support them; the more readily support; the the believers; the support; the the more; the worthy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the because that for his name's sake they went forth, the taking nothing of the Gentiles, and the self-denying preachers. The verse thus records the ground for supporting the self-denying preachers — teaching that the brethren went out for Christ's name and refused heathen support, so the believers ought to support them.
8:
"We therefore ought to receive such, that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth." The witnesses note the duty to receive such preachers and so be fellow-helpers to the truth. Gill notes "we ought to receive such" preachers of the word into our houses, and entertain them cheerfully while they continue, and supply them with all necessaries when they depart. The witnesses note John declaring that we ought to receive such, that we might be fellow-helpers to the truth; the duty of hospitality to the Lord's servants, making the host a partner in the gospel — that John draws the conclusion from the brethren's self-denial (v.7): "we therefore ought to receive such" (it is our duty to welcome and support such preachers, receiving them into our homes and supplying their needs) — "that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth"; the witnesses note the honour of this service — those who receive and help the travelling preachers become "fellowhelpers to the truth," that is, partners and co-workers in the spread of the gospel; though they do not themselves go out to preach, by supporting those who do, they share in the work and its reward; so that hospitality to the Lord's servants is no small thing, but a real fellowship in the cause of the truth; we therefore ought to receive such, that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth. The witnesses note we therefore ought to receive such. The witnesses note that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth.

The witnesses note the duty to receive the Lord's servants, that the host becomes a "fellowhelper to the truth" — a partner in the gospel, sharing the work and its reward by supporting those who preach.

So the witnesses make the verse hospitality making the host a partner in the gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is that those who receive and support the preachers become fellow-helpers to the truth, sharing in the work. The witnesses note the receive such: "we therefore ought to receive such" — Gill notes welcome and support such preachers; the receive such; the welcome; the support; the the preachers; the the homes. The expositors observe the supply needs: receiving them and supplying their needs; the supply needs; the receive; the their needs; the the homes; the cheerfully. The witnesses press the fellowhelpers: "that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth" — partners and co-workers in the gospel; the fellowhelpers; the partners; the co-workers; the the gospel; the the truth. The expositors draw the lesson on the share the work: though not preaching, they share in the work; the share the work; the not preaching; the the work; the by supporting; the the reward. The witnesses observe the and reward: they share the work and its reward; the and reward; the the work; the the reward; the the cause; the partners. The expositors note the no small thing: hospitality to the Lord's servants is no small thing; the no small thing; the hospitality; the the Lord's servants; the fellowship; the the truth. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we therefore ought to receive such, the that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth, and the partner in the gospel. The verse thus records hospitality making the host a partner — teaching that those who receive and support the preachers become fellow-helpers to the truth.
9:
"I wrote unto the church: but Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence among them, receiveth us not." The witnesses note Diotrephes, who loved preeminence and rejected the apostle. Gill notes John wrote to the church where Gaius was a member, some church in Asia near Ephesus; but Diotrephes, who loved preeminence, received not the apostle nor his letter. The witnesses note John saying that he wrote to the church, but Diotrephes, who loves to have the preeminence among them, receives them not; the ambitious church-leader who rejected the apostle's authority — that John turns to a sad contrast with the faithful Gaius: "I wrote unto the church" (John had sent a letter to the church) "but Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence among them, receiveth us not"; the witnesses note the character laid bare — Diotrephes was a man in the church (perhaps a leader) marked by ambition, who "loveth to have the preeminence" (craved the first place, to rule and be chief above all) — and from this proud root sprang his sin: he "receiveth us not," refusing to acknowledge or submit to John and the brethren the apostle commended, rejecting even apostolic authority; so that the love of preeminence is named as the spring of his unruly and divisive conduct; Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence among them, receiveth us not. The witnesses note I wrote unto the church. The witnesses note Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence among them, receiveth us not.

The witnesses note Diotrephes, the ambitious church-leader who "loveth to have the preeminence" and rejected the apostle — the love of preeminence the spring of his unruly, divisive conduct.

So the witnesses make the verse Diotrephes rejecting apostolic authority from ambition. The lesson the expositors draw is that Diotrephes, loving the first place, refused to receive John and the brethren he commended. The witnesses note the wrote to the church: "I wrote unto the church" — Gill notes John sent a letter; the wrote to the church; the a letter; the John; the the church; the in Asia. The expositors observe the Diotrephes: "but Diotrephes" — a man in the church, perhaps a leader; the Diotrephes; the a man; the a leader; the the church; the ambitious. The witnesses press the loveth preeminence: "who loveth to have the preeminence among them" — craved the first place; the loveth preeminence; the the first place; the to rule; the chief; the ambition. The expositors draw the lesson on the proud root: from this proud root sprang his sin; the proud root; the the sin; the ambition; the the spring; the pride. The witnesses observe the receiveth us not: "receiveth us not" — refused John's authority; the receiveth us not; the refused; the John; the the brethren; the apostolic authority. The expositors note the unruly conduct: the spring of his unruly, divisive conduct; the unruly conduct; the divisive; the the spring; the preeminence; the sin. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I wrote unto the church, the but Diotrephes who loveth to have the preeminence among them receiveth us not, and Diotrephes' ambition. The verse thus records Diotrephes rejecting apostolic authority from ambition — teaching that Diotrephes, loving the first place, refused to receive John and the brethren.
10:
"Wherefore, if I come, I will remember his deeds which he doeth, prating against us with malicious words: and not content therewith, neither doth he himself receive the brethren, and forbiddeth them that would, and casteth them out of the church." The witnesses note the catalogue of Diotrephes' offences and John's resolve to deal with them. Gill notes that if John comes, where both Gaius and Diotrephes lived, he will remember his deeds; Barnes notes he would punish his arrogance and presumption. The witnesses note John resolving that, if he comes, he will remember Diotrephes' deeds — his malicious prating, his refusing the brethren, forbidding others, and casting them out; the fourfold wickedness of Diotrephes and the apostle's purpose to confront it — that John lays out the full account of Diotrephes' wrongdoing and his resolve to deal with it: "wherefore, if I come, I will remember his deeds which he doeth"; the witnesses note the rising scale of his sin — first, "prating against us with malicious words" (idle, slanderous talk against the apostle and the brethren) — and "not content therewith," he went further: he "neither doth he himself receive the brethren" (refusing hospitality to the travelling preachers), "and forbiddeth them that would" (hindering others who wished to receive them), "and casteth them out of the church" (excommunicating those who showed such hospitality); so that his ambition issued in slander, inhospitality, the forbidding of others, and tyrannical expulsion — a sad picture of unchecked pride in the church, which the apostle would openly confront; if I come, I will remember his deeds which he doeth. The witnesses note prating against us with malicious words. The witnesses note neither doth he himself receive the brethren, and forbiddeth them that would, and casteth them out of the church.

The witnesses note Diotrephes' fourfold wickedness — malicious prating, refusing the brethren, forbidding others, and casting them out — and John's resolve, if he comes, to confront it openly.

So the witnesses make the verse the catalogue of Diotrephes' sin and John's resolve. The lesson the expositors draw is that Diotrephes' ambition issued in slander, inhospitality, and tyranny, which the apostle would confront. The witnesses note the remember his deeds: "if I come, I will remember his deeds" — Barnes notes he would deal with his arrogance; the remember his deeds; the deal with; the arrogance; the confront; the the deeds. The expositors observe the prating: "prating against us with malicious words" — slanderous talk; the prating; the slanderous; the malicious; the words; the against the apostle. The witnesses press the receive not the brethren: "neither doth he himself receive the brethren" — refusing hospitality; the receive not; the the brethren; the refusing; the hospitality; the the preachers. The expositors draw the lesson on the forbiddeth: "and forbiddeth them that would" — hindering others who would; the forbiddeth; the hindering; the others; the who would; the prevent. The witnesses observe the casteth out: "and casteth them out of the church" — excommunicating the hospitable; the casteth out; the excommunicating; the the hospitable; the the church; the tyranny. The expositors note the rising scale: a rising scale of sin from ambition; the rising scale; the the sin; the ambition; the slander; the tyranny. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the prating against us with malicious words, the neither doth he himself receive the brethren and forbiddeth them that would and casteth them out of the church, and Diotrephes' offences. The verse thus records the catalogue of Diotrephes' sin and John's resolve — teaching that Diotrephes' ambition issued in slander, inhospitality, and tyranny, which the apostle would confront.
11:
"Beloved, follow not that which is evil, but that which is good. He that doeth good is of God: but he that doeth evil hath not seen God." The witnesses note the charge to follow the good, not the evil, with its test of true godliness. Gill notes "follow not that which is evil" in general, it being hateful to God and contrary to his nature and will; and particularly imitate not the evils in Diotrephes — his pride, ambition, and love of preeminence. The witnesses note John charging the beloved to follow not evil but good, since he that does good is of God, but he that does evil has not seen God; the call to imitate the good, not Diotrephes' evil, with the mark of the true child of God — that John, drawing the lesson from the bad example, exhorts Gaius and all: "beloved, follow not that which is evil, but that which is good" (do not imitate the evil, such as Diotrephes' pride and tyranny, but copy the good, such as Gaius's hospitality) — and he gives the searching test: "he that doeth good is of God: but he that doeth evil hath not seen God"; the witnesses note the dividing rule — the habitual doer of good shows himself to be "of God" (born of and belonging to God), while the doer of evil "hath not seen God" (has no true knowledge or experience of God, whatever he professes); so that conduct reveals the heart, and the one who does evil, like Diotrephes, gives proof he does not truly know God; follow not that which is evil, but that which is good. The witnesses note follow not that which is evil, but that which is good. The witnesses note he that doeth good is of God: but he that doeth evil hath not seen God.

The witnesses note the charge to follow good, not evil — not imitating Diotrephes; the doer of good is of God, the doer of evil has not seen God; conduct revealing the heart.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to follow good, with its test of godliness. The lesson the expositors draw is that the doer of good is of God, while the doer of evil shows he has not truly known God. The witnesses note the follow not evil: "follow not that which is evil, but that which is good" — Gill notes imitate not Diotrephes' evil but the good; the follow not evil; the imitate not; the Diotrephes; the the good; the copy. The expositors observe the the example: drawing the lesson from the bad example; the the example; the the bad; the the lesson; the Diotrephes; the Gaius. The witnesses press the doeth good is of God: "he that doeth good is of God" — the doer of good belongs to God; the doeth good is of God; the the doer of good; the of God; the born of; the belonging. The expositors draw the lesson on the doeth evil: "he that doeth evil hath not seen God" — the doer of evil has not known God; the doeth evil; the not seen God; the no knowledge; the no experience; the professes. The witnesses observe the conduct reveals: conduct reveals the heart; the conduct reveals; the the heart; the the deeds; the the proof; the shows. The expositors note the Diotrephes proves: Diotrephes, doing evil, proves he does not know God; the Diotrephes proves; the not know God; the shows. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he that doeth good is of God but he that doeth evil hath not seen God, and the call to follow good. The verse thus records the call to follow good with its test — teaching that the doer of good is of God, while the doer of evil shows he has not known God.
12:
"Demetrius hath good report of all men, and of the truth itself: yea, and we also bear record; and ye know that our record is true." The witnesses note the threefold good testimony to Demetrius. Gill notes Demetrius was of a quite different cast from Diotrephes, and therefore the apostle mentions him to Gaius to be followed, not the other; he was perhaps the bearer of this letter. The witnesses note John commending Demetrius, who has good report of all men, and of the truth itself, and of the apostle, whose witness is true; the threefold witness to the good Demetrius, set against Diotrephes — that John holds up a third figure, a good example to set beside the bad: "Demetrius hath good report of all men, and of the truth itself: yea, and we also bear record"; the witnesses note the threefold testimony to his character — first, "of all men" (a good report from all who knew him, even the world acknowledging his worth) — second, "of the truth itself" (his life agreed with the truth, so that the gospel itself, as it were, bore witness to him, his conduct matching its standard) — and third, "we also bear record" (the apostle's own testimony, added and confirmed: "ye know that our record is true"); so that Demetrius, unlike Diotrephes, was a man of approved and well-attested godliness, worthy to be followed; Demetrius hath good report of all men, and of the truth itself. The witnesses note Demetrius hath good report of all men, and of the truth itself. The witnesses note we also bear record; and ye know that our record is true.

The witnesses note the threefold witness to Demetrius — of all men, of the truth itself, and of the apostle — a good example set against Diotrephes, a man of approved and well-attested godliness.

So the witnesses make the verse the threefold testimony to the good Demetrius. The lesson the expositors draw is that Demetrius, unlike Diotrephes, had a threefold good report and was worthy to be followed. The witnesses note the good report of all: "Demetrius hath good report of all men" — Gill notes a good report from all who knew him; the good report of all; the all men; the all who knew; the worth; the acknowledged. The expositors observe the set against Diotrephes: a good example set against Diotrephes; the set against Diotrephes; the the bad; the the good; the the contrast; the followed. The witnesses press the of the truth: "and of the truth itself" — his life agreed with the truth; the of the truth; the his life; the agreed; the the gospel; the matched. The expositors draw the lesson on the we bear record: "yea, and we also bear record" — the apostle's own testimony; the we bear record; the the apostle; the testimony; the added; the confirmed. The witnesses observe the our record true: "ye know that our record is true" — his witness is true; the our record true; the his witness; the true; the confirmed; the attested. The expositors note the well-attested: Demetrius was a man of approved, well-attested godliness; the well-attested; the approved; the godliness; the worthy; the followed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Demetrius hath good report of all men and of the truth itself, the we also bear record and ye know that our record is true, and the threefold witness. The verse thus records the threefold testimony to Demetrius — teaching that Demetrius, unlike Diotrephes, had a threefold good report and was worthy to be followed.
13:
"I had many things to write, but I will not with ink and pen write unto thee:" The witnesses note John's preference to speak in person rather than write all. Gill notes John had many things to write, with regard to churches, particular persons, and hospitality to the poor brethren. Barnes notes the epistle closes, as the second does, with a statement that he had many things to say but preferred waiting till he should see him. The witnesses note John saying that he had many things to write, but would not write them with ink and pen; the apostle's many things deferred to a personal meeting — that John, drawing to a close as in his second epistle, says he has more to communicate but chooses not to commit it all to writing: "I had many things to write, but I will not with ink and pen write unto thee"; the witnesses note the reason, which the next verse gives — John had much on his heart concerning churches, persons, and the care of the poor brethren, but rather than set it all down "with ink and pen," he preferred to reserve it for a personal meeting, where heart could speak to heart more freely and fully than letters allow; so that the brevity of the letter is not from lack of matter, but from the hope of a near and fuller conversation face to face; I had many things to write, but I will not with ink and pen write unto thee. The witnesses note I had many things to write. The witnesses note but I will not with ink and pen write unto thee.

The witnesses note John's many things deferred to a personal meeting — not committing all to ink and pen, but reserving it for a face-to-face conversation where heart could speak to heart more fully.

So the witnesses make the verse the many things deferred to a meeting. The lesson the expositors draw is that John had much to say but preferred to reserve it for a personal meeting rather than write it all. The witnesses note the many things: "I had many things to write" — Gill notes much concerning churches and persons; the many things; the churches; the persons; the the poor; the much. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes he preferred waiting till he should see him; the Barnes; the preferred waiting; the see him; the the meeting; the deferred. The witnesses press the not with ink: "but I will not with ink and pen write unto thee" — chose not to commit all to writing; the not with ink; the not write; the all; the deferred; the reserved. The expositors draw the lesson on the personal meeting: he preferred a personal meeting; the personal meeting; the face to face; the reserved; the the conversation; the fuller. The witnesses observe the heart to heart: where heart could speak to heart more fully; the heart to heart; the more fully; the freely; the than letters; the the conversation. The expositors note the not lack of matter: the brevity is not from lack of matter; the not lack of matter; the the brevity; the not lack; the the hope; the fuller. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I had many things to write, the but I will not with ink and pen write unto thee, and the deferred meeting. The verse thus records the many things deferred to a meeting — teaching that John had much to say but preferred to reserve it for a personal meeting.
14:
"But I trust I shall shortly see thee, and we shall speak face to face. Peace be to thee. Our friends salute thee. Greet the friends by name." The witnesses note the hope of a personal meeting and the closing greetings. Gill notes John trusts he shall shortly see Gaius, either at Ephesus or at the place where Gaius lived. Barnes notes "our friends salute thee" — that is, your friends and mine, referring to their mutual private friends. The witnesses note John trusting that he will shortly see Gaius and speak face to face, with the benediction of peace and the exchange of friendly greetings; the hope of a face-to-face meeting and the warm, personal close — that John ends the letter with the same warmth that fills it: "but I trust I shall shortly see thee, and we shall speak face to face" (the hoped-for personal visit, when they would talk together freely) — "Peace be to thee" (the benediction of peace upon his friend) — "Our friends salute thee. Greet the friends by name"; the witnesses note the tender, personal note of the close — the mutual friends of John and Gaius send their greetings, and John bids Gaius "greet the friends by name," each one individually, a touch of warm, particular affection; so that this little letter closes, as it began, in the warm fellowship and personal love of the Christian family; Peace be to thee. Our friends salute thee. Greet the friends by name. The witnesses note I trust I shall shortly see thee, and we shall speak face to face. The witnesses note Peace be to thee. Our friends salute thee. Greet the friends by name.

The witnesses note the hope of a face-to-face meeting and the warm, personal close — peace, mutual friends' greetings, and the charge to "greet the friends by name," each one individually.

So the witnesses make the verse the hope of meeting and the warm close. The lesson the expositors draw is that John ends with the hope of a personal visit, the benediction of peace, and the exchange of friendly greetings. The witnesses note the see thee: "I trust I shall shortly see thee, and we shall speak face to face" — Gill notes the hoped-for visit; the see thee; the the visit; the face to face; the talk; the freely. The expositors observe the hoped-for: the hoped-for personal meeting; the hoped-for; the personal; the the visit; the shortly; the face to face. The witnesses press the peace: "Peace be to thee" — the benediction of peace; the peace; the the benediction; the upon his friend; the the blessing; the peace. The expositors draw the lesson on the our friends: "Our friends salute thee" — Barnes notes mutual friends' greetings; the our friends; the mutual; the friends; the greetings; the salute. The witnesses observe the greet by name: "Greet the friends by name" — each one individually; the greet by name; the each one; the individually; the by name; the particular. The expositors note the warm fellowship: the warm fellowship and personal love of the family; the warm fellowship; the personal love; the the family; the the close; the affection. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I trust I shall shortly see thee and we shall speak face to face, the Peace be to thee Our friends salute thee Greet the friends by name, and the warm close. The verse thus records the hope of meeting and the warm close — teaching that John ends with the hope of a personal visit, the benediction of peace, and friendly greetings.
